identity.short_name: CRT-defined $T$ with odd prime-power and $2$-power components; valuation/order forcing; quotient sum mod $1000$ with a noninvertible divisor

problem_spec.summary: A CRT-defined integer $T$ is constrained modulo $11^5$, $19^2$, and $2^{10}$. The minimal exponent $t$ with $T^t\equiv 1$ simultaneously is forced by (i) a valuation formula near $1$ at $11^5$, (ii) order lifting from $19$ to $19^2$, and (iii) the exact order of $3$ modulo $2^{10}$. A sum of three integer quotients is then computed modulo $1000$, including a quotient by $2^{10}$ handled via residues mod $8$ and $125$.
givens: $T$ is the unique integer with $0\le T<M$ such that $T\equiv 1+4\cdot 11^2\pmod{11^5}$, $T\equiv 2\pmod{19^2}$, and $T\equiv 3\pmod{2^{10}}$.
$t$ is the smallest positive integer such that $11^5\mid (T^t-1)$, $19^2\mid (T^t-1)$, and $2^{10}\mid (T^t-1)$.
$N$ is the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv A\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: odd_prime_power_1
parameter(s).domain: an odd prime power $p^5$ with a residue $T\equiv 1+u p^2\pmod{p^5}$ and $p\nmid u$
parameter(s).purpose: forces a large exact order via a valuation formula

parameter(s).name: odd_prime_square
parameter(s).domain: an odd prime square $q^2$ with a residue class $T\equiv g\pmod{q^2}$ where $\operatorname{ord}_q(g)=q-1$ and $g^{q-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$
parameter(s).purpose: forces full order $q(q-1)$ by order lifting

parameter(s).name: two_power
parameter(s).purpose: forces a precise $2$-power order via $2$-adic valuation growth

parameter(s).name: output_modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final normalization modulus for the quotient sum

invariant(s).name: CRT_uniqueness_three_moduli
invariant(s).statement: The moduli $p^5$, $q^2$, and $2^k$ are pairwise coprime, so CRT gives a unique class modulo $M$, and $0\le T<M$ fixes the representative.

invariant(s).name: valuation_order_forcing_at_p5
invariant(s).statement: If $T\equiv 1+u p^2\pmod{p^5}$ with $p\nmid u$ and $p$ odd, then $v_p(T^n-1)=2+v_p(n)$, forcing $\operatorname{ord}_{p^5}(T)=p^3$.

invariant(s).name: invertible_divisors_mod_1000
invariant(s).statement: $\gcd(p^5,1000)=\gcd(q^2,1000)=1$, so the quotients by $p^5$ and $q^2$ reduce modulo $1000$ via modular inverses.

invariant(s).name: mixed_division_for_two_power
invariant(s).statement: For $K=(T^t-1)/2^k$, compute $K\bmod 8$ from $T^t\bmod 2^{k+3}$ and compute $K\bmod 125$ from $T^t\bmod 125$ (since $2^k$ is invertible mod $125$); CRT uniquely determines $K\bmod 1000$.

essential_assumption(s): The three moduli are pairwise coprime (CRT well-posedness).
$p\nmid u$ so $v_p(T-1)=2$ and valuation forcing is nondegenerate.
$\gcd(T,1000)=1$ so exponent reduction modulo $100$ is valid.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT and $0\le T<M$ give a unique $T$.
Valuation forcing at $p^5$, order lifting at $q^2$, and the $2$-adic order lemma uniquely determine local orders and thus uniquely force $t$ as an $\operatorname{lcm}$.
Odd-denominator quotient residues are unique via modular inverses; the $2^k$-denominator quotient residue is uniquely determined via residues mod $8$ and $125$ and CRT.
Normalization $0\le N<1000$ makes the final answer unique.

technique(s).name: Chinese_remainder_theorem_construction
inputs: a congruence system modulo three pairwise coprime prime powers
outputs: a unique $T$ modulo $M$ and residues of $T$ modulo smaller moduli

technique(s).name: order_lifting_prime_to_square
inputs: a unit with $\operatorname{ord}_q(T)=q-1$ and a check of $T^{q-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{q^2}$

theorem(s).name: basic_group_order_divisibility
theorem(s).role: order divides $\varphi(m)$ and lifts by divisibility under reduction maps

theorem(s).name: CRT_mod_8_125
theorem(s).role: recombines residues modulo $8$ and $125$ into a unique residue modulo $1000$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: local multiplicative order controls divisibility of $T^t-1$ by prime powers

critical_trick(s): force $t$ by combining three different order computations (valuation forcing, order lifting to a prime square, and $2$-adic order)
handle division by $2^{10}$ modulo $1000$ via mixed residues modulo $8$ and $125$

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: CRT, modular inverses, multiplicative orders, and independent checks

task(s).name: CRT_and_order_verification
task(s).reason: Verify $T$ satisfies the three congruences and confirm the three local multiplicative orders.

task(s).name: quotient_sum_mod_1000_check
task(s).reason: Verify the three quotient residues modulo $1000$ via modular arithmetic, including the noninvertible $2^{10}$ case by using modulus $1024\cdot 1000$.
